keyMyths.title: Genesis in Enuma Elish
keyMyths.description: In Babylonian mythology, the Enuma Elish begins with Apsu (fresh water) and Tiamat (salt water) coexisting, representing the primordial waters from which all gods and the cosmos emerge. Their conflict and Tiamat's subsequent defeat by Marduk leads to the creation of the world from her body.

keyMyths.title: The Egyptian Nun
keyMyths.description: In Egyptian cosmology, Nun is the personification of the primordial watery abyss from which all things arose. Atum, the creator god, emerges from Nun to begin the process of creation, separating order from chaos.

keyMyths.title: Ymir's Blood in Norse Mythology
keyMyths.description: In Norse mythology, the cosmos is formed from the body of the giant Ymir. When Odin and his brothers slay Ymir, his blood floods the world, creating the oceans and seas, representing a violent and transformative origin from primordial substance.

keyMyths.title: The Cosmic Ocean in Hindu Mythology
keyMyths.description: In Hindu mythology, Vishnu rests on the serpent Shesha, floating on the Kshira Sagara, the cosmic ocean of milk, between creations. This symbolizes the potentiality and unmanifest state before the universe is manifested.

keyMyths.title: The Waters Above and Below in Genesis
keyMyths.description: The Book of Genesis describes God separating the waters above from the waters below, creating the sky and the seas. This act of division establishes order from a formless, watery chaos, marking the beginning of creation.

sources.name: Leeming, David Adams. Creation Myths of the World.
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of creation myths from various cultures, often highlighting the role of primordial waters.
sources.title: Leeming, David Adams. Creation Myths of the World.

sources.name: Eliade, Mircea. Cosmos and History: The Myth of the Eternal Return.
sources.description: Explores the symbolic significance of creation myths and the role of primordial elements in shaping human understanding of time and existence.
sources.title: Eliade, Mircea. Cosmos and History: The Myth of the Eternal Return.

sources.name: Sandars, N.K. The Epic of Gilgamesh.
sources.description: Provides context for understanding the Enuma Elish and its place within Mesopotamian cosmology.
sources.title: Sandars, N.K. The Epic of Gilgamesh.

sources.name: Pritchard, James B., ed. Ancient Near Eastern Texts Relating to the Old Testament.
sources.description: A collection of primary source texts, including the Enuma Elish and other creation accounts from the ancient Near East.
sources.title: Pritchard, James B., ed. Ancient Near Eastern Texts Relating to the Old Testament.

sources.name: Campbell, Joseph. The Hero With a Thousand Faces.
sources.description: Explores the archetypal motifs present in mythology, including the recurring theme of creation from chaos and the symbolic importance of water.
sources.title: Campbell, Joseph. The Hero With a Thousand Faces.
